Daily DAP Accessibility Report — 2026-03-18#

Run ID: run-2026-03-18-2590a9970e · Status: success · Source data: 2026-03-16 · Generated: 2026-03-18T15:06:45.821Z

About These Reports#

The Digital Analytics Program (DAP) is a U.S. government analytics service that collects website traffic data across participating federal agencies. DAP tracks page views, visitor counts, and usage patterns for hundreds of government websites, providing transparency into how the public engages with federal digital services.

This report measures the quality and accessibility of the top 100 most-visited U.S. government URLs as reported by DAP. Each day, Lighthouse scans are run against these URLs to measure:

  • Performance - How fast pages load for users (scores 0-100, higher is better)
  • Accessibility - How well pages work for users with disabilities, following WCAG guidelines (scores 0-100, higher is better)
  • Best Practices - Whether pages follow modern web development standards (scores 0-100, higher is better)
  • SEO - How well pages are optimized for search engines (scores 0-100, higher is better)

Accessibility findings come from axe-core, the industry-standard accessibility testing engine embedded in Lighthouse. The axe findings surface specific WCAG violations such as missing alternative text, insufficient color contrast, and missing form labels that make government websites harder to use for people with disabilities.

Traffic data reflects daily visitor counts from DAP. URLs are ranked by page load count, ensuring the most-used government pages are prioritized for quality measurement.

Accessibility Trend Narrative#

Over the past 21 days of data (2026-02-26 to 2026-03-18), government website accessibility scores have improved (+24.26 points, from 68 to 92.26). Performance scores have improved (+15.96 points, from 38 to 53.96).

Today's aggregate accessibility score of 92.12 reflects the mean Lighthouse accessibility score across 100 successfully scanned government URLs. A score above 90 indicates generally strong compliance with WCAG automated checks, though manual testing is always recommended to fully assess accessibility.

Disability icon key
Without Vision — People who are blind or have no functional vision (1.0% of U.S. population — ~3,400,000 Americans)
Limited Vision — People with low vision who need magnification or high contrast (2.4% of U.S. population — ~8,100,000 Americans)
Without Perception of Color — People who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness) (4.3% of U.S. population — ~14,500,000 Americans)
Without Hearing — People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content (0.3% of U.S. population — ~1,100,000 Americans)
Limited Hearing — People with hearing loss who may struggle with audio without accommodations (3.5% of U.S. population — ~11,900,000 Americans)
Without Speech — People who cannot use speech or voice-based input effectively (0.5% of U.S. population — ~1,700,000 Americans)
Limited Manipulation — People with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity (2.2% of U.S. population — ~7,600,000 Americans)
Limited Reach and Strength — People with limited reach, strength, or stamina (5.8% of U.S. population — ~19,600,000 Americans)
Limited Language, Cognitive, and Learning Abilities — People with cognitive, learning, or language differences (4.7% of U.S. population — ~15,900,000 Americans)

These icons show which groups of people with disabilities are excluded by each accessibility barrier. Where page view data is available, each icon shows an estimated number of people potentially excluded (page loads for affected URLs × disability prevalence rate from U.S. Census ACS 2022). Hover over or focus an icon to see the full estimate and methodology. Icons follow the Section 508 Functional Performance Criteria and the equivalent EU EN 301 549 v3.2.1 Table B.2 categories.

Prevalence data: U.S. Census Bureau, American Community Survey (ACS) 2022 1-Year Estimates, Table B18101; supplemental data from CDC, NIDCD, AFB, and NIH/NEI. U.S. population base: ~335,900,000 (2022 estimate). Reviewed annually.

See the Section 508 WCAG to FPC mapping for additional detail on how accessibility requirements map to functional needs.

Estimated Impact (daily)#

No accessibility findings data available for this scan. The impact estimation requires detailed accessibility findings from scanning tools. Currently, the scanner is running in a mode that does not collect individual accessibility issues.

Americans Excluded by Disability#

No accessibility findings data is available to estimate excluded users by disability category for this scan.

Performance Impact on Americans#

Google defines a good Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) as under 2.5 seconds and recommends pages under 1.6 MB. The figures below estimate how much extra time Americans spend waiting, and how much extra data is transferred, because government websites fall short of these benchmarks. Calculations are based on 98 successfully scanned URLs with Lighthouse timing data.

Estimated performance impact vs. Google benchmarks for today's scanned government URLs
Metric Estimated total (today) Notes
Extra time waiting (vs 2.5s LCP benchmark) 3,785.4 days 327,056,835 seconds
Extra data transferred (vs 1.6 MB page weight benchmark) 62,496.82 GB Across 98 URLs with weight data

Extra time is calculated as: for each scanned URL, max(0, actual LCP − 2.5s) × page loads. Extra data is calculated as: max(0, actual page weight − 1.6 MB) × page loads. LCP and page weight are measured by Lighthouse. These are rough estimates based on a sample of the top government URLs by traffic.

Accessibility Findings

https://tools.usps.com

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 68

Axe findings: 10

`button`, `link`, and `menuitem` elements do not have accessible names. (rule: aria-command-name)

When an element doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it with a generic name, making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make command elements more accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~126.9KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~126,930 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 12,693,022 affected page loads) ~38.1KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~38,079 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 12,693,022 affected page loads) ~279.2KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~279,246 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 12,693,022 affected page loads)

Affected elements (3):

Element 1

Element path: div.panel-heading > h4.panel-title > a#headingOneAnchor > span.down-arr

Snippet:

<span role="button" data-parent="#accordion" aria-expanded="true" aria-controls="collapseOne" class="down-arr">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute

Element 2

Snippet:

<span role="button" data-parent="#accordion" aria-expanded="true" aria-controls="collapseOne" class="down-arr">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute

  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty

Assistive technologies, like screen readers, can't interpret ARIA attributes with invalid values. Learn more about valid values for ARIA attributes.

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order)

List items (`<li>`) are not contained within `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` parent elements. (rule: listitem)

Screen readers require list items (`<li>`) to be contained within a parent `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` to be announced properly. Learn more about proper list structure.

Accessibility Findings

https://forecast.weather.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 61

Axe findings: 6

Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio. (rule: color-contrast)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Learn how to provide sufficient color contrast.

Disabilities affected: ~78.4KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~78,442 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 3,268,432 affected page loads) ~140.5KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Element path: div.one-sixth-first > div#forecast-lookup > form#getForecast > input#inputstring

Snippet:

<input id="inputstring" name="inputstring" type="text" value="Enter location ..." onclick="this.value=''" autocomplete="off">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element has insufficient color contrast of 2.84 (foreground color: #999999, background color: #ffffff, font size: 8.0pt (10.6667px), font weight: normal). Expected contrast ratio of 4.5:1
`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang)

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

Accessibility Findings

https://usps.com

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 68

Axe findings: 9

`[aria-*]` attributes do not match their roles (rule: aria-allowed-attr)

Each ARIA `role` supports a specific subset of `aria-*` attributes. Mismatching these invalidates the `aria-*` attributes. Learn how to match ARIA attributes to their roles.

Estimated ~23,066 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 2,306,642 affected page loads) ~6.9KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Element 1

Element path: div#usps-carousel > div.carousel-inner > ol.carousel-indicators > li.active

Snippet:

<li data-target="#usps-carousel" data-slide-to="0" aria-checked="true" class="active">

Label: Slide 1

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • ARIA attribute is not allowed: aria-checked="true"

Element 2

Element path: div#usps-carousel > div.carousel-inner > ol.carousel-indicators > li

Snippet:

<li data-target="#usps-carousel" data-slide-to="2" aria-checked="false">

Label: Slide 3

Element path: div#usps-carousel > div.carousel-inner > ol.carousel-indicators > li

Snippet:

<li data-target="#usps-carousel" data-slide-to="3" aria-checked="false">

Label: Slide 4

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • ARIA attribute is not allowed: aria-checked="false"
Elements with an ARIA `[role]` that require children to contain a specific `[role]` are missing some or all of those required children. (rule: aria-required-children)

Some ARIA parent roles must contain specific child roles to perform their intended accessibility functions. Learn more about roles and required children elements.

`[role]`s are not contained by their required parent element (rule: aria-required-parent)

Some ARIA child roles must be contained by specific parent roles to properly perform their intended accessibility functions. Learn more about ARIA roles and required parent element.

Image elements do not have `[alt]` attributes (rule: image-alt)

Informative elements should aim for short, descriptive alternate text. Decorative elements can be ignored with an empty alt attribute. Learn more about the `alt` attribute.

  • Element does not have an alt attribute
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty

  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"
Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name)

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

List items (`<li>`) are not contained within `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` parent elements. (rule: listitem)

Screen readers require list items (`<li>`) to be contained within a parent `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` to be announced properly. Learn more about proper list structure.

`[user-scalable="no"]` is used in the `<meta name="viewport">` element or the `[maximum-scale]` attribute is less than 5. (rule: meta-viewport)

Disabling zooming is problematic for users with low vision who rely on screen magnification to properly see the contents of a web page. Learn more about the viewport meta tag.

Disabilities affected: ~55.4KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~55,359 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 2,306,642 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: head > meta

Snippet:

<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0, maximum-scale=1.0, user-scalable=no">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • user-scalable=no on <meta> tag disables zooming on mobile devices

`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang)

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

Disabilities affected: ~20.1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~20,077 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 2,007,675 affected page loads) ~6.0KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~6,023 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 2,007,675 affected page loads) ~94.4KPeople with cognitive, learning, or language differences. Estimated ~94,361 people potentially excluded (4.7% prevalence × 2,007,675 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: html

Snippet:

<html xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1999/xhtml">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The <html> element does not have a lang attribute
Image elements do not have `[alt]` attributes (rule: image-alt)

Informative elements should aim for short, descriptive alternate text. Decorative elements can be ignored with an empty alt attribute. Learn more about the `alt` attribute.

  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty

  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

  • Element has no title attribute

Select elements do not have associated label elements. (rule: select-name)

Form elements without effective labels can create frustrating experiences for screen reader users. Learn more about the `select` element.

Elements with an ARIA `[role]` that require children to contain a specific `[role]` are missing some or all of those required children. (rule: aria-required-children)

Some ARIA parent roles must contain specific child roles to perform their intended accessibility functions. Learn more about roles and required children elements.

Some ARIA child roles must be contained by specific parent roles to properly perform their intended accessibility functions. Learn more about ARIA roles and required parent element.

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order)

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang)

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 22.6px instead of at least 24px.

Image elements do not have `[alt]` attributes (rule: image-alt)

Informative elements should aim for short, descriptive alternate text. Decorative elements can be ignored with an empty alt attribute. Learn more about the `alt` attribute.

Disabilities affected: ~12.8KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~12,778 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 1,277,759 affected page loads) ~3.8KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Element 1

Element path: div > div.blazy > div.media > img.media__element

Snippet:

<img decoding="async" class="media__element b-lazy" loading="lazy" src="" width="480" height="360">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have an alt attribute
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"
Elements with visible text labels do not have matching accessible names. (rule: label-content-name-mismatch)

Visible text labels that do not match the accessible name can result in a confusing experience for screen reader users. Learn more about accessible names.

Accessibility Findings

https://travel.state.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 93

Axe findings: 2

`[user-scalable="no"]` is used in the `<meta name="viewport">` element or the `[maximum-scale]` attribute is less than 5. (rule: meta-viewport)

Disabling zooming is problematic for users with low vision who rely on screen magnification to properly see the contents of a web page. Learn more about the viewport meta tag.

Disabilities affected: ~23.1KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~23,120 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 963,339 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: head > meta

Snippet:

<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0, maximum-scale=1.0, user-scalable=no">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • user-scalable=no on <meta> tag disables zooming on mobile devices
Document does not have a main landmark. (rule: landmark-one-main)

One main landmark helps screen reader users navigate a web page. Learn more about landmarks.

Estimated ~21,193 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 963,339 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: html

Snippet:

<html lang="en">

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • Document does not have a main landmark

Download full axe findings JSON | Download full axe findings CSV

Accessibility Findings

https://radar.weather.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 77

Axe findings: 2

`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang)

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

Disabilities affected: ~8.6KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~8,556 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 855,596 affected page loads) ~2.6KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~2,567 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 855,596 affected page loads) ~40.2KPeople with cognitive, learning, or language differences. Estimated ~40,213 people potentially excluded (4.7% prevalence × 855,596 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: html

Snippet:

<html>

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The <html> element does not have a lang attribute
`[user-scalable="no"]` is used in the `<meta name="viewport">` element or the `[maximum-scale]` attribute is less than 5. (rule: meta-viewport)

Disabling zooming is problematic for users with low vision who rely on screen magnification to properly see the contents of a web page. Learn more about the viewport meta tag.

Disabilities affected: ~20.5KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~20,534 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 855,596 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: head > meta

Snippet:

<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1, maximum-scale=1, user-scalable=0">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • user-scalable on <meta> tag disables zooming on mobile devices

Download full axe findings JSON | Download full axe findings CSV

Accessibility Findings

https://cdc.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 91

Axe findings: 3

Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name)

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~8.1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~8,073 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 807,259 affected page loads) ~2.4KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~2,422 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 807,259 affected page loads) ~17.8KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~17,760 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 807,259 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: nav.mobile-navbar > div.mobile-navbar_top > div.d-flex > a#cdc-mobile-search-toggle

Snippet:

<a id="cdc-mobile-search-toggle" class="nav-link" href="#" aria-controls="cdc-mobile-search-form">

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • Element is in tab order and does not have accessible text
  • Fix any of the following:
  • Element does not have text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
Skip links are not focusable. (rule: skip-link)

Including a skip link can help users skip to the main content to save time. Learn more about skip links.

Disabilities affected: ~8.1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~8,073 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 807,259 affected page loads) ~2.4KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Element 1

Element path: body.cdc-post-type--cdc-homepage > div#skipmenu > a.skippy

Snippet:

<a class="skippy visually-hidden-focusable" href="#headerSearch" tabindex="2">

Label: Skip to search

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • No skip link target
Some elements have a `[tabindex]` value greater than 0 (rule: tabindex)

A value greater than 0 implies an explicit navigation ordering. Although technically valid, this often creates frustrating experiences for users who rely on assistive technologies. Learn more about the `tabindex` attribute.

Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio. (rule: color-contrast)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Learn how to provide sufficient color contrast.

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order)

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Disabilities affected: ~7.2KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~7,235 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 723,487 affected page loads) ~2.2KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~15,917 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 723,487 affected page loads)

  • Heading order invalid
Links rely on color to be distinguishable. (rule: link-in-text-block)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Link text that is discernible improves the experience for users with low vision. Learn how to make links distinguishable.

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 97

Lists do not contain only `<li>` elements and script supporting elements (`<script>` and `<template>`). (rule: list)

Screen readers have a specific way of announcing lists. Ensuring proper list structure aids screen reader output. Learn more about proper list structure.

Accessibility Findings

https://urs.earthdata.nasa.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 81

Axe findings: 5

Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name)

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~6.5KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~6,453 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 645,276 affected page loads) ~1.9KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~1,936 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 645,276 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: div#tophat3Widget > nav#tophat3WidgetNavBar > div.container-fluid > button#tophat3HamburgerButton

Snippet:

<button class="navbar-toggler" type="button" id="tophat3HamburgerButton">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have inner text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

`<td>` elements in a large `<table>` do not have table headers. (rule: td-has-header)

Screen readers have features to make navigating tables easier. Ensuring that `<td>` elements in a large table (3 or more cells in width and height) have an associated table header may improve the experience for screen reader users. Learn more about table headers.

Disabilities affected: ~5.2KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 97

Axe findings: 1

Touch targets do not have sufficient size or spacing. (rule: target-size)

Touch targets with sufficient size and spacing help users who may have difficulty targeting small controls to activate the targets. Learn more about touch targets.

Disabilities affected: ~11.5KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~11,463 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 521,051 affected page loads) ~30.2KPeople with limited reach, strength, or stamina. Estimated ~30,221 people potentially excluded (5.8% prevalence × 521,051 affected page loads)

Affected elements (15):

Element 1

Element path: div.row > div.col-xs-12 > sup > a#password-tip

Snippet:

<a role="button" id="password-tip" tabindex="0" data-placement="top" data-remotecontent="/entreg/assets/html/tooltips/tool-tip-forgot-useralias.html" data-toggle="popover" aria-label="Retrieve your username" data-bs-original-title="Retrieve your username">

Label: Retrieve your username

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size (12px by 15px, should be at least 24px by 24px)

Accessibility Findings

https://egov.uscis.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 90

Axe findings: 1

The document uses `<meta http-equiv="refresh">` (rule: meta-refresh)

Users do not expect a page to refresh automatically, and doing so will move focus back to the top of the page. This may create a frustrating or confusing experience. Learn more about the refresh meta tag.

Disabilities affected: ~4.6KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~4,622 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 462,201 affected page loads) ~1.4KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~1,387 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 462,201 affected page loads) ~10.2KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~10,168 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 462,201 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: head > meta

Snippet:

<meta http-equiv="refresh" content="360">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • <meta> tag forces timed refresh of page (less than 20 hours)

Accessibility Findings

https://tsdr.uspto.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 87

Axe findings: 4

Elements with `role="dialog"` or `role="alertdialog"` do not have accessible names. (rule: aria-dialog-name)

ARIA dialog elements without accessible names may prevent screen readers users from discerning the purpose of these elements. Learn how to make ARIA dialog elements more accessible.

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: body > div.QSIWebResponsive > div.QSIWebResponsive-creative-container-fade > div.QSIWebResponsiveDialog-Layout1-SI_3P4z2eDgSVwaaFd_content

<div class="QSIWebResponsiveDialog-Layout1-SI_3P4z2eDgSVwaaFd_content QSIWebResponsive…" role="dialog" aria-modal="true" aria-labelledby="QSIWebResponsiveDialog-Layout1-SI_3P4z2eDgSVwaaFd-headline" aria-describedby="QSIWebResponsiveDialog-Layout1-SI_3P4z2eDgSVwaaFd-description" style="background-color: rgb(255, 255, 255); opacity: 1;">

Label: Help us improve the Trademark Status and Document Retrieval (TSDR) by providing…

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio. (rule: color-contrast)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Learn how to provide sufficient color contrast.

Estimated ~10,478 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 436,572 affected page loads) ~18.8KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~18,773 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 436,572 affected page loads)

Some elements have a `[tabindex]` value greater than 0 (rule: tabindex)

A value greater than 0 implies an explicit navigation ordering. Although technically valid, this often creates frustrating experiences for users who rely on assistive technologies. Learn more about the `tabindex` attribute.

Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name)

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

https://tsa.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 99

Axe findings: 1

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order)

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Accessibility Findings

https://science.nasa.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 95

Axe findings: 1

Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name)

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~3.9KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~3,929 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 392,915 affected page loads) ~1.2KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Element path: header.hds-nasa-header > div.usa-nav-container > div.grid-col > button#global-navigation-trigger--mobile

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have inner text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>

  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

Some elements have a `[tabindex]` value greater than 0 (rule: tabindex)

A value greater than 0 implies an explicit navigation ordering. Although technically valid, this often creates frustrating experiences for users who rely on assistive technologies. Learn more about the `tabindex` attribute.

Accessibility Findings

https://spc.noaa.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 53

Axe findings: 8

Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio. (rule: color-contrast)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Learn how to provide sufficient color contrast.

`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang)

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

Image elements do not have `[alt]` attributes (rule: image-alt)

Informative elements should aim for short, descriptive alternate text. Decorative elements can be ignored with an empty alt attribute. Learn more about the `alt` attribute.

Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name)

Touch targets do not have sufficient size or spacing. (rule: target-size)

Touch targets with sufficient size and spacing help users who may have difficulty targeting small controls to activate the targets. Learn more about touch targets.

Document does not have a main landmark. (rule: landmark-one-main)

One main landmark helps screen reader users navigate a web page. Learn more about landmarks.

Disabilities affected: ~3.4KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~3,413 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 341,304 affected page loads) ~1.0KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~1,024 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 341,304 affected page loads) ~7.5KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~7,509 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 341,304 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: html

Snippet:

<html xmlns="//www.w3.org/1999/xhtml">

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • Document does not have a main landmark
`<td>` elements in a large `<table>` do not have table headers. (rule: td-has-header)

Screen readers have features to make navigating tables easier. Ensuring that `<td>` elements in a large table (3 or more cells in width and height) have an associated table header may improve the experience for screen reader users. Learn more about table headers.

Disabilities affected: ~3.4KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~3,413 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 341,304 affected page loads) ~1.0KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~1,024 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 341,304 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: tbody > tr > td > table

Snippet:

<table cellspacing="1" cellpadding="2" border="0" width="100%">

Label: Hazard Wed (03/18) Thu (03/19) Fri (03/20) Sat (03/21) Sun (03/22) Mon (03/23) …

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • Some non-empty data cells do not have table headers

Accessibility Findings

https://ceac.state.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 94

Axe findings: 2

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order)

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Disabilities affected: ~3.2KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~3,249 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 324,863 affected page loads) ~975People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~7,147 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 324,863 affected page loads)

Element 1

Element path: div.category > div#visa > div.section > h4

Snippet:

<h4>

Label: NONIMMIGRANT

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Heading order invalid
Some elements have a `[tabindex]` value greater than 0 (rule: tabindex)

A value greater than 0 implies an explicit navigation ordering. Although technically valid, this often creates frustrating experiences for users who rely on assistive technologies. Learn more about the `tabindex` attribute.

Disabilities affected: ~3.2KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~3,249 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 324,863 affected page loads) ~975People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Accessibility Findings

https://tsp.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 91

Axe findings: 2

Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name)

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~3.2KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Links rely on color to be distinguishable. (rule: link-in-text-block)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Link text that is discernible improves the experience for users with low vision. Learn how to make links distinguishable.

Accessibility Findings

https://my.uscis.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 89

Axe findings: 2

`[aria-*]` attributes do not match their roles (rule: aria-allowed-attr)

Each ARIA `role` supports a specific subset of `aria-*` attributes. Mismatching these invalidates the `aria-*` attributes. Learn how to match ARIA attributes to their roles.

Estimated ~2,871 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 287,111 affected page loads) ~861People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Element path: div.uscis-row > div.col-span-6 > div.mobile-header > a#toggle-sections

Label: Menu

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • ARIA attribute is not allowed: aria-expanded="false"
`[user-scalable="no"]` is used in the `<meta name="viewport">` element or the `[maximum-scale]` attribute is less than 5. (rule: meta-viewport)

Disabling zooming is problematic for users with low vision who rely on screen magnification to properly see the contents of a web page. Learn more about the viewport meta tag.

Disabilities affected: ~6.9KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~6,891 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 287,111 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: head > meta

Snippet:

<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width,height=device-height,user-scalable=no,initial-scale=1.0…">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • user-scalable=no on <meta> tag disables zooming on mobile devices

Accessibility Findings

https://eeoc.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 96

Axe findings: 2

Some elements have a `[tabindex]` value greater than 0 (rule: tabindex)

A value greater than 0 implies an explicit navigation ordering. Although technically valid, this often creates frustrating experiences for users who rely on assistive technologies. Learn more about the `tabindex` attribute.

Lists do not contain only `<li>` elements and script supporting elements (`<script>` and `<template>`). (rule: list)

Screen readers require list items (`<li>`) to be contained within a parent `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` to be announced properly. Learn more about proper list structure.

Accessibility Findings

https://acf.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 97

Axe findings: 1

Touch targets do not have sufficient size or spacing. (rule: target-size)

Touch targets with sufficient size and spacing help users who may have difficulty targeting small controls to activate the targets. Learn more about touch targets.

Disabilities affected: ~4.9KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~4,911 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 223,217 affected page loads) ~12.9KPeople with limited reach, strength, or stamina. Estimated ~12,947 people potentially excluded (5.8% prevalence × 223,217 affected page loads)

Affected elements (8):

Element 1

Element path: li > ul > li > a.external

Snippet:

<a href="https://www.hhs.gov" class="external" target="_blank">

Label: HHS.gov

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size because it is partially obscured (smallest space is 59.4px by 19.2px, should be at least 24px by 24px)
  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 15.8px instead of at least 24px.

Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name)

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~2.2KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~2,211 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 221,090 affected page loads) ~663People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have inner text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

Image elements do not have `[alt]` attributes (rule: image-alt)

Informative elements should aim for short, descriptive alternate text. Decorative elements can be ignored with an empty alt attribute. Learn more about the `alt` attribute.

Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name)

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

List items (`<li>`) are not contained within `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` parent elements. (rule: listitem)

Screen readers require list items (`<li>`) to be contained within a parent `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` to be announced properly. Learn more about proper list structure.

Accessibility Findings

https://sam.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 97

Axe findings: 2

Elements use prohibited ARIA attributes (rule: aria-prohibited-attr)

Using ARIA attributes in roles where they are prohibited can mean that important information is not communicated to users of assistive technologies. Learn more about prohibited ARIA roles.

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: div.paragraph__column > div.px-video-container > div#progress-bar > div#progress

Snippet:

<div class="progress" id="progress" aria-label="Video: " style="width: 0%;">

Label: Video:

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • aria-label attribute cannot be used on a div with no valid role attribute.
Elements with visible text labels do not have matching accessible names. (rule: label-content-name-mismatch)

Visible text labels that do not match the accessible name can result in a confusing experience for screen reader users. Learn more about accessible names.

Accessibility Findings

https://eric.ed.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 69

Axe findings: 4

Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio. (rule: color-contrast)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Learn how to provide sufficient color contrast.

Disabilities affected: ~4.4KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~4,386 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 182,745 affected page loads) ~7.9KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~7,858 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 182,745 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: div#sbar > form#f > div > input

Snippet:

<input type="submit" value="Search">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element has insufficient color contrast of 4.11 (foreground color: #ffffff, background color: #357ae8, font size: 10.0pt (13.3333px), font weight: bold). Expected contrast ratio of 4.5:1
`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang)

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The <html> element does not have a lang attribute

`[aria-*]` attributes do not match their roles (rule: aria-allowed-attr)

Each ARIA `role` supports a specific subset of `aria-*` attributes. Mismatching these invalidates the `aria-*` attributes. Learn how to match ARIA attributes to their roles.

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 94

Axe findings: 2

`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang)

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

List items (`<li>`) are not contained within `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` parent elements. (rule: listitem)

Screen readers require list items (`<li>`) to be contained within a parent `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` to be announced properly. Learn more about proper list structure.

Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name)

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>

`[user-scalable="no"]` is used in the `<meta name="viewport">` element or the `[maximum-scale]` attribute is less than 5. (rule: meta-viewport)

Disabling zooming is problematic for users with low vision who rely on screen magnification to properly see the contents of a web page. Learn more about the viewport meta tag.

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • user-scalable=no on <meta> tag disables zooming on mobile devices

Accessibility Findings

https://fbi.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 79

Axe findings: 5

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order)

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Disabilities affected: ~1.3KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~1,297 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 129,668 affected page loads) ~389People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~389 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 129,668 affected page loads) ~2.9KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~2,853 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 129,668 affected page loads)

Element path: div.g-4 > div.col-6 > div.quick-link > h6

Snippet:

<h6>

Label: Apply for an FBI Job

Element 2

Element path: div.container > div.row > div.col-md-3 > h5.mb-3

Snippet:

<h5 class="mb-3">

Label: Most Wanted

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Heading order invalid
Image elements do not have `[alt]` attributes (rule: image-alt)

Informative elements should aim for short, descriptive alternate text. Decorative elements can be ignored with an empty alt attribute. Learn more about the `alt` attribute.

Disabilities affected: ~1.3KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~1,297 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 129,668 affected page loads) ~389People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~389 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 129,668 affected page loads)

Affected elements (2):

Element 1

Element path: div.top-nav-bar > div.top-nav-bar-bottom > div.left > img

Snippet:

<img src="https://www.fbi.gov/++theme++fbigov.theme/uswds-2.9.0/img/icon-dot-gov.svg">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have an alt attribute
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

Element 2

Element path: div.top-nav-bar > div.top-nav-bar-bottom > div.right > img

Snippet:

<img src="https://www.fbi.gov/++theme++fbigov.theme/uswds-2.9.0/img/icon-https.svg">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have an alt attribute
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"
Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name)

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

Touch targets do not have sufficient size or spacing. (rule: target-size)

Touch targets with sufficient size and spacing help users who may have difficulty targeting small controls to activate the targets. Learn more about touch targets.

Disabilities affected: ~2.9KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~2,853 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 129,668 affected page loads) ~7.5KPeople with limited reach, strength, or stamina. Estimated ~7,521 people potentially excluded (5.8% prevalence × 129,668 affected page loads)

Affected elements (4):

Element 1

Element path: section.hero-section > div.hero-indicators > div.d-flex > button.indicator

Snippet:

<button class="indicator active" aria-label="Slide 1" data-slide="1">

Label: Slide 1

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size (12px by 12px, should be at least 24px by 24px)
  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 16px instead of at least 24px.

Element 2

Element path: section.hero-section > div.hero-indicators > div.d-flex > button.indicator

Snippet:

<button class="indicator" aria-label="Slide 2" data-slide="2">

Label: Slide 2

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size (12px by 12px, should be at least 24px by 24px)
  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 16px instead of at least 24px.

Element 3

Element path: section.hero-section > div.hero-indicators > div.d-flex > button.indicator

Snippet:

<button class="indicator" aria-label="Slide 3" data-slide="3">

Label: Slide 3

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size (12px by 12px, should be at least 24px by 24px)
  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 16px instead of at least 24px.

Element 4

Element path: section.hero-section > div.hero-indicators > div.d-flex > button.indicator

Snippet:

<button class="indicator" aria-label="Slide 4" data-slide="4">

Label: Slide 4

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size (12px by 12px, should be at least 24px by 24px)
  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 16px instead of at least 24px.

`[user-scalable="no"]` is used in the `<meta name="viewport">` element or the `[maximum-scale]` attribute is less than 5. (rule: meta-viewport)

Disabling zooming is problematic for users with low vision who rely on screen magnification to properly see the contents of a web page. Learn more about the viewport meta tag.

Disabilities affected: ~3.1KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~3,105 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 129,393 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: head > meta

Snippet:

<meta name="viewport" content="user-scalable=no,initial-scale=1,maximum-scale=1,minimum-scale=1,width=dev…">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • user-scalable=no on <meta> tag disables zooming on mobile devices

Elements with visible text labels do not have matching accessible names. (rule: label-content-name-mismatch)

Accessibility Findings

https://i94.cbp.dhs.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 90

Axe findings: 5

`[aria-hidden="true"]` elements contain focusable descendents (rule: aria-hidden-focus)

Focusable descendents within an `[aria-hidden="true"]` element prevent those interactive elements from being available to users of assistive technologies like screen readers. Learn how `aria-hidden` affects focusable elements.

Disabilities affected: ~1.3KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Some elements have a `[tabindex]` value greater than 0 (rule: tabindex)

A value greater than 0 implies an explicit navigation ordering. Although technically valid, this often creates frustrating experiences for users who rely on assistive technologies. Learn more about the `tabindex` attribute.

List items (`<li>`) are not contained within `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` parent elements. (rule: listitem)

Tables do not use `<caption>` instead of cells with the `[colspan]` attribute to indicate a caption. (rule: table-fake-caption)

Screen readers have features to make navigating tables easier. Ensuring that tables use the actual caption element instead of cells with the `[colspan]` attribute may improve the experience for screen reader users. Learn more about captions.

Image elements do not have `[alt]` attributes (rule: image-alt)

Informative elements should aim for short, descriptive alternate text. Decorative elements can be ignored with an empty alt attribute. Learn more about the `alt` attribute.

  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty

Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name)

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

Touch targets do not have sufficient size or spacing. (rule: target-size)

Touch targets with sufficient size and spacing help users who may have difficulty targeting small controls to activate the targets. Learn more about touch targets.

Affected elements (3):

Element 1

Element path: div.row-fluid > div.span12 > div.links > a

Snippet:

<a href="https://www.fdrlibrary.org/events-calendar">

Label: eNews

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size (45px by 14px, should be at least 24px by 24px)
  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 20px instead of at least 24px.
